content:
Each new AI model lands as a smaller leap than the last That’s not in my head, and the boring reason might be the best news my job’s had in a while.

5 min read
Just now

--

The AI is slowing down fight is everywhere right now. From a working dev’s chair, both sides are half right, and the half that wins is the calm one.
I noticed it around the last model upgrade. A new frontier model dropped, the benchmarks were up a couple of points, the launch post used the word break through, and I plugged it into my actual workflow and felt, for the first time in about three years, nothing. No oh, it can do that now. No rearranging how I work.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

It was a bit better at a couple of things and completely identical at everything I actually do all day. And I sat there waiting for the wow, and the wow didn’t come.
For a second I thought it was me. Hype fatigue, a jaded palate. Then I looked up and noticed half the internet is having the exact same argument this week.
AI is slowing down is genuinely the hottest fight in tech right now, hundreds of people in the comments going at each other, and the strange part is that both camps are partly right.

The part that really is slowing
Here’s what’s actually true, and it isn’t nothing. The engine that made 2020 through 2023 feel like a rocket was stupidly simple. Make the model bigger, feed it more of the internet, watch it get smarter.
It worked so well it started to feel like a law of physics. The trouble is you can’t run that play forever, and we’re hitting the edge of it from two directions at once.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One, the raw size growth basically stopped. Frontier model parameters have been creeping up maybe five percent a year since 2021. Between 2019 and 2021 they grew by more than a hundred times. That’s less a slowdown than a different sport.
Two, and this is the real wall, we are running out of internet. The whole trick depended on an endless supply of high-quality human text to train on, and there is only so much of the stuff.
The estimates vary, but the consensus is that frontier models are on track to have effectively read all the good text humans have ever written somewhere in the next handful of years. You can buy more GPUs.
You cannot buy a second internet. Even Ilya Sut skever, who would know, has said the gains from just scaling up have plateaued.
So when people say the easy magic is over, they’re right. The just make it bigger era is genuinely ending.

The part that absolutely isn’t
But “the old engine ran out of fuel” is not the same sentence as “the car stopped,” and this is where the doom half of the argument loses me. The labs didn’t hit the wall and go home. They swapped engines.
The whole frontier moved to other axes the second pure scaling got expensive. Test-time compute, letting a model think longer on a hard problem instead of just being fatter.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Reasoning models that work through steps. Mixture-of-experts setups, post-training with reinforcement learning, synthetic data, and a genuinely surprising trend where small models trained cleverly are catching giant ones from a year ago. Anthropic, for what it’s worth, says it still hasn’t seen scaling laws break at all. The benchmark numbers are, in fact, still climbing. They’re just climbing because of craft now instead of brute mass.
So AI is slowing down is quietly doing a sleight of hand. One specific technique, pure pre-training scale, is hitting diminishing returns. Progress as a whole picked a different road.
Those are not the same claim, and the headline blurs them on purpose, because the dominant scaling approach is maturing while the field diversifies its methods does not get 600 comments.

Why the plateau might be the good ending
Here’s the turn, the bit I actually wanted to write about. If the felt rate of change really is flattening out at the level where I work, I think that might be the best thing to happen to this job in years, and almost nobody is framing it that way.
Think about what the last three years actually cost you. Not the model getting better, that part was great.
The exhaustion. The thing where you’d learn a workflow, get genuinely good at it, build habits around a tool, and eight weeks later the ground would move and half of it was obsolete and you were a beginner again at the thing you’d just gotten competent at. The treadmill. The low background hum of everything I know expires this quarter.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A plateau means the tool stops moving under your feet. It means you get to learn the current generation properly, deeply, instead of forever surfing the next release.
It means the advantage drifts back from who adopted the newest model fastest toward who actually understands their problem, which is the kind of edge that doesn’t evaporate on a Tuesday. Stable tools are when people finally build the good stuff, because you can plan past the end of the month.

So where I land
I’m not calling the top. People have called the AI plateau before and looked a bit silly six months later when some new unlock landed, and the data wall might get walked around by synthetic data or by something nobody’s thought of yet. I’m holding it all loosely.
But I would gently push back on both the doomers and the boosters. To the it’s over crowd, no, the impact has barely started, because most of the value never lived in a smarter model in the first place.
It lives in the slow boring work of actually wiring the current one into real products, and that’s a multi-year job that doesn’t need GPT-anything to improve to keep paying out.
And to the it’s still exponential, keep up or die crowd, I’d just ask when your own work last genuinely changed, honestly, as opposed to when you last read that it had.
Anyway I didn’t upgrade to the newest model on my main setup this month. first time I’ve skipped one in years went and got properly good at the one I’ve already got instead, and my output went up, not down, which tells me something fairly pointed about where the bottleneck actually was this whole time…
